project: Advanced GANs for the Holistic Restoration of Historical Manuscripts

records.content: Prof. Miller, I've drafted my proposal for restoring historical manuscripts using GANs. My goal is to build an unsupervised CycleGAN that can 'restore' any faded, torn, or damaged document from any collection. f1.pdf is the first version.

Chris, unsupervised CycleGAN is a good first step, but historical manuscripts are extremely diverse. You can't expect a single model to learn the aging process of both parchment and rag paper. You need to narrow the scope. Also, standard GANs struggle with physical tears—they often 'hallucinate' content that isn't semantically consistent. I suggest focusing on 18th-century English manuscripts first and looking into inpainting techniques like Partial Convolutions.

project: Advanced GANs for the Holistic Restoration of Historical Manuscripts

records.content: I've revised the plan to focus on 18th-century English manuscripts. I'm focusing specifically on 'hole filling' (tears) using Partial Convolutions to avoid V1's hallucination problem. f2.pdf specifies the inpainting architecture.

The inpainting in f2.pdf is much better in terms of visual fidelity. However, for historical research, visual 'plausibility' is not enough. If your model turns a faded 'e' into an 'o', you're introducing historical errors. How can we ensure semantic consistency? You should explore coupling the generator with a pre-trained OCR engine to use semantic recognition as a secondary loss. This will ensure the restored text is actually correct.
